content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are playing a game. They have with them a binary string SSS.

Alice and Bob alternate making moves, with Alice going first.

On their turn, a player does the following:


Choose a non-empty contiguous substring of SSS all of whose characters are the same, delete it from SSS, and concatenate the remaining parts.

More formally, choose integers LLL and RRR such that 1≤L≤R≤∣S∣1 \leq L \leq R \leq |S|1≤L≤R≤∣S∣ and SL=SL+1=…=SRS_L = S_{L+1} = \ldots = S_RSL​=SL+1​=…=SR​, and replace SSS with the string S1S2…SL−1SR+1SR+2…S∣S∣S_1S_2\ldots S_{L-1} S_{R+1}S_{R+2}\ldots S_{|S|}S1​S2​…SL−1​SR+1​SR+2​…S∣S∣​.

This reduces the length of SSS by R−L+1R-L+1R−L+1.


Alice wins immediately when SSS doesn't contain any occurrence of 111, while Bob wins immediately when SSS doesn't contain any occurrence of 000. Both players will play to win.

Note that if the string initially doesn't contain any occurrences of 000, Bob wins before any moves are made.

Bob wants to win the game, so before the game starts, he can flip some characters of SSS.

That is, Bob can choose an index iii (1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N), and set SiS_iSi​ to 000 if it was originally 111, and vice versa.

Find the **minimum** number of flips Bob needs to make to ensure he can win.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the length of SSS.
The second line contains the binary string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of flips required in SSS to make Bob win.
constraints: SSS is a binary string, i.e, contains only the characters 000 and 111.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Bob changes the only character of SSS, resulting in S=1S = \texttt{1}S=1.
There are no 000's in this string, so Bob wins automatically.
Test case 222: There are no 000's in the string, so Bob wins without having to change anything.
Test case 333: Bob can flip the first character of the string, turning it into 111001\texttt{111001}111001.
It can be shown that if the game is played on this string, Bob will win; whereas he would lose if the game is played on the initial string.
